title: Spinning from a raw mixed fleece
subtitle: Scouring, grading, and processing an ungraded farm fleece before spinning
excerpt: An ungraded farm fleece contains several different grades of wool from the same animal: fine shoulder wool, coarser britch wool from the back legs, and the varying neck and belly sections. Scouring removes lanolin and vegetable matter; then each section needs assessing separately. The best sections go to the drum carder or hand cards for spinning; the rest suits insulation batting or garden mulch.
sourceNotes: Fleece grading and scouring methods for handspinners are documented in guild resources, small-flock shepherd guidance, and spinning texts including The Fleece and Fiber Sourcebook. The shoulder-vs-britch distinction and the use of sub-grade fleece for insulation are standard practice in small-flock fleece handling.

glossaryTerms.definition: The natural waxy grease produced by the sebaceous glands of sheep and secreted into the fleece. Lanolin coats the fibre surface and protects the sheep from rain and cold. In raw fleece it makes the fibres heavy and sticky; it must be washed out before spinning unless you are spinning in the grease deliberately.

The length of a lock of wool from tip to cut end, measured in centimetres. Staple length varies considerably across different body regions of the same fleece: shoulder sections typically have the longest, finest staple; britch sections have shorter, coarser staple.

The process of removing the heavily soiled, short, or matted edges of a freshly shorn fleece before rolling it for sale or storage. Skirted sections include belly wool, leg wool, and the dung-contaminated edges. A well-skirted fleece from a farm will still contain some variation in quality between regions.

content.text: Buying a fleece direct from a farm or smallholder is one of the most economical ways to source spinning fibre. An ungraded fleece requires more preparation work than commercial top or roving. The fleece from a single animal is not uniform. Shoulder wool is typically the finest and longest. Britch wool from the back legs is coarser and shorter. Neck and belly sections fall somewhere between the two and may contain more vegetable matter or second cuts.
Before any spinning or carding can happen, the
lanolin
and grease must be washed out. After washing, grading the fleece by section tells you what you are working with and prevents you from spending time carding fibre that is better used in the garden.
What you need

items.name: Washing-up liquid or dedicated wool scour
Two large buckets or a clean sink

content.text: Step 1: Initial assessment and skirting

Lay the fleece on a clean surface and pull it open gently. Check for the worst of the edge material before scouring: any sections with hard dung tags, very short locks under 4 cm (second cuts from the shearer), or heavy burr and seed contamination should be removed now. These sections are
skirting
material. Set them aside for the compost or insulation pile.

Weigh the fleece before scouring and record it. A raw fleece typically loses 20-40% of its weight in washing: a 2 kg raw fleece may yield 1.2-1.6 kg of scoured fibre, depending on the breed and the condition of the fleece.
Step 2: Scouring

Fill a bucket or sink with hot water: as hot as your tap allows, ideally 60-70 degrees C. Add a generous squirt of washing-up liquid. Do not use wool wash here; the point is to cut through lanolin grease, and a detergent works better than a gentle soap for this step.

Load the fleece into a mesh laundry bag and submerge it without agitating. Agitation in hot water is what felts wool: the goal is to let the hot soapy water dissolve the grease passively. Leave for 20-25 minutes. Lift the bag, let it drain, and transfer to a second bucket of equally hot clean water for a rinse. Repeat if the rinse water runs brown.

Press the fleece in a towel to remove excess water and spread it on a drying rack or a clean fleece. Air dry fully before carding. Damp fibre fed onto a drum carder transfers moisture to the drum's cloth and can rust the teeth.
Step 3: Grading the scoured fleece
Once dry, divide the fleece into three rough grades. Check the
staple length
and softness of a representative lock from each region. Shoulder wool: the longest, finest locks. This is the spinning grade. Set it aside for drum carding or hand carding into top-quality yarn.

Neck and side wool: moderate staple length, potentially more vegetable matter. Worth picking through: remove any matted tips (weathered tips that have felted at the end of the lock) by holding the lock and pulling gently to separate the felted tip. The remaining lock is usable. If more than a third of the lock is matted, set it aside.

Britch wool: coarser, shorter staple from the back legs. Check by rubbing a lock against your inner wrist: if it prickles noticeably, it is unlikely to make comfortable next-to-skin yarn. Britch wool suits outer-garment yarns (socks, outerwear, working mittens), felting projects, or garden mulch. Do not discard it if it has reasonable staple length: it may be exactly right for a hard-wearing project.
Step 4: Processing for spinning

For the spinning-grade fleece, choose between drum carding (for a lofty, woollen-draw result) or hand carding (for a shorter-staple or finer preparation). Pick out any remaining vegetable matter by hand before loading onto either card. A few passes will produce rolags or a batt ready for the wheel.
Common problems

items.cause: Temperature shock between the wash water and the rinse water, or the fleece was agitated while hot.
items.fix: Match the rinse water temperature to the wash water as closely as possible. Move the fleece in and out of water without squeezing or scrubbing. If partial felting has occurred, gently tease the affected sections apart while the fleece is still warm and damp.

items.problem: The scoured fleece still feels greasy
items.cause: Insufficient detergent, or the wash temperature was not hot enough to dissolve the lanolin.
items.fix: Repeat the scouring step with fresh hot water and a larger quantity of washing-up liquid. Some breeds have higher lanolin content (Merino, Rambouillet) and may need two full scours.

items.cause: Seed heads and grass stems that are caught in the fibre rather than sitting on the surface are difficult to card out.
items.fix: Pick out large pieces before carding. Very fine seeds (grass seeds) can sometimes be removed by combing the carded preparation; others are impossible to fully remove and the fleece section is better set aside for felting projects where the VM will be encased.
